title: Shannon Rumsey
text: Hi! my name is Shannon Rumsey and I am an Undergraduate Researcher at the University of California, Santa Barbara. While I am well-versed in the world of data science, my specialty and passion is computational linguistics and natural language processing. You can find more about me professionally and personally on the about me page.

title: about me
text:
I am currently wrapping up my final year at the University of California, Santa Barbara, where I am studying Statistics and Data Science B.S. Here, I have taken a wide range of various statistics courses including Intermediate Python, Regression Analysis, and anything in between.
While at UCSB, I had the opportunity to work on my first research paper titled Identifying Optimal Case Onset Points for Early Detection of Influenza-like Illness. For this project, I worked alongside a group of 4 and a data science team at Evidation Health Inc. for 6 months. At the end, I participated in two project showcases, one on campus and another at the KDD 2023 conference in Long Beach.








KDD 2023 conference in Long Beach.

While working on my first research project, I was sponsored by the NSF to promote the field of data science within my community. As part of my job, I curated numerous event posters and aided a professor with one of their course websites.
Currently, I am in my last quarter and in the transition period of applying to graduate school. While tying up loose ends, I am pursuing my interests in the interdisciplinary field of Linguistics and Statistics, though many know this as “natural language processing”. Current research at the CPLS lab includes the exploration and validation of the Functional Load Hypothesis and it’s relation to predicting phonological mergers.

The Functional Load Hypothesis (Jakobson, 1931; Martinet, 1952) states that sounds distinguishing more words (high functional load) are less likely to disappear or merge with other sounds during sound change (UCLA)

title: vitae
text:
I believe that data science is similar to the art of painting, both use different forms of expression to communicate an idea to a broad audience. As a statistician, my duty is to prioritize open-communication and storytelling to ensure that statistics and machine learning are accessible to everyone, just like painting. My beliefs allow me to be a diligent worker who has a passion for building connections, both interpersonal and mathematically, and helping to create a world that everyone is apart of and can feel happy about.



Technical Skills

Languages
R, Python, SQL, HTML, CSS, Excel, SAS


Machine Learning
Tree Algorithms: XGBoost, Random Forests, Decision Trees
Regression: Logistic Regression, Linear Regression, Lasso Regression, Ridge Regression
Clustering: K-Nearest Neighbors
Deep Learning: Neural Networks, Recurrent Neural Networks, Encoder Models, Decoder Models, Encoder-Decoder Models


Computational Linguistics
Hidden Markov Models, Noisy Channel models, Large Language Models (Bert and GPT)
Working with word vectors and cosine similarity to achieve similarity scores
Observing semantic relationships
Recognizing trends within text data in order to extrapolate predictions




Education
University of California, Santa Barbara | B.S. in Statistics and Data Science, GPA: 3.5 (2021 - 2023)
Santa Barbara City College | A.A. in Liberal Arts-Science: Science & Math, GPA: 3.86 (2019 - 2021)


Experience

Undergraduate Research Assistant
UC Santa Barbara (2023 - Present)
Conducting research alongside a professor, PhD candidate, and fellow undergraduate student with the goal of validating and building upon 3+ prior linguistic research papers proving the Functional Load Hypothesis. The research is conducted at the CPLS Lab at the University of California, Santa Barbara.


Researcher
Evidation Health, Inc. (2023)
Spokesperson and meeting-organizer for a team of five students, two industry sponsors, and an academic adviser. Learned methods for working with time-series health data to establish trends as well as determine how to handle missing data with the use of data imputation techniques in R and
Python. Developed two innovative models to define respiratory illness case onset and recovery period.


Central Coast Data Science Fellow
National Science Foundation (2022-2023)
As a fellow, my goal was to advance data science education and understanding in the community. I was responsible for outreach efforts to promote data science courses and events, as well as engage with community members to raise awareness about the importance of data science in today’s world. I produced two flyers to promote a course and a project showcase. I worked alongside my professor to help build a course website utilizing quarto and R.




Awards
HDR DSC Award #1924205 and Award #1924008, issued by the National Science Foundation’s Harnessing the Data Revolution Data Science Corps
